replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz Sprinter 209-319 CDI Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market ranges across all Sprinter CDI generations. A reconditioned OM642 V6 supply-only costs around £3,800, with full supply and fit climbing to approximately £5,900. A typical engine replacement in the UK can range from £3,067 to £6,105 or more. See the Era Map above for generation-specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Sprinter CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £75–£110/hr (independent Mercedes commercial specialist) or £130–£180/hr (London/SE dealer). The engine bay is spacious on the Sprinter, but the V6 (OM642) is tighter.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential repair — diesel smell/carbon buildup is serious
Forced regen or cleaning — worth it

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2013 OM651 with documented timing chain history
* Any Sprinter with full service history and regular oil changes (8,000–10,000 miles)
* OM646-powered van (2006–2013) with injector seal history and no diesel smell
* Regular long-distance usage pattern (DPF-friendly) rather than urban-only
* OM642 with documented EGR and emissions system history
avoidIf: * OM651 with any cold-start rattle — timing chain stretch is serious and expensive
* OM642 with EGR warnings or hesitation — V6 emissions repairs are costly
* Any Sprinter with diesel smell in the cabin — injector seal failure
* Poor service history — all CDI engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Sprinter with DPF warning light without documented repair — emissions repairs are expensive

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Sprinter 209-319 CDI range covers the workhorse diesel engines that built Mercedes' commercial reputation. The OM646 (2006–2013) is a robust, simple engine but injector seal leaks ("black death") are the defining issue. The OM651 (2013–2018) is fundamentally reliable — 200,000–300,000 km examples are common — but timing chain stretch from ~120,000 km is the critical failure point. The OM642 V6 offers more power but has a reputation for expensive emissions system failures. Buy on history, maintain the timing chain and emissions systems, and the Sprinter will reward you.

cta.label: → Compare Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for any rattle on startup (OM651 timing chain stretch warning)
* Service History — Check the year to identify which engine — see the Era Map above
* Diesel Smell — Any diesel smell in the cabin? (injector seal failure — "black death")
* Injector Area — Check for black carbon residue around injectors (visible leak)
* DPF History — Ask about driving patterns; frequent short trips cause DPF issues
* EGR System — Any hesitation or rough running? (EGR clogging)
* Coolant Level — Check for any loss — fuel filter housing (OM651) or water pump leaks
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? DPF/EGR codes are common
* Rust Check — Sprinter chassis, sills and wheel arches are prone to rust

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only, varying by generation: OM646 £2,500–£4,500, OM651 £3,000–£5,500, OM642 £4,000–£7,000. A reconditioned OM642 V6 supply-only costs around £3,800, with full supply and fit climbing to approximately £5,900. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Check the Era Map above for your engine code.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine reliable?
items.answer: That depends on which engine and generation. The OM646 (2006–2013) is robust but injector seal leaks ("black death") are common. The OM651 (2013–2018) is fundamentally reliable — 200,000–300,000 km examples are common — but timing chain stretch from ~120,000 km is the defining risk. The OM642 V6 is powerful but has a reputation for expensive emissions system failures. Buy on history and maintain the timing chain and emissions systems.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI?
items.answer: OM651: timing chain stretch and guide wear (cold-start rattle, £1,500–£3,500). OM646: injector seal failure (diesel smell, black carbon residue, £400–£1,000). OM642: EGR valve clogging (hesitation, Check Engine Light, £500–£1,200). All: DPF and EGR system problems (£400–£1,200), fuel filter housing coolant leaks (OM651, £300–£600), water pump leaks (£400–£800).

items.question: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine, or should I scrap the van?
items.answer: Sprinter values range from £4,000–£20,000 depending on generation, condition and mileage. A £3,500–£6,500 reconditioned engine represents 20–80% of vehicle value — our 70% Mercedes-Benz scrap threshold means careful evaluation is required. For a well-maintained Sprinter with a solid chassis, replacement is often justified. For an ageing van with significant rust or other structural issues, assess scrap value first.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Sprinter CDI engine code?
items.answer: The Sprinter CDI has used three engine families: OM646 (2006–2013, 2.2L, 87–163 bhp), OM651 (2013–2018, 2.1L, 95–190 bhp), and OM642 (2006–2018, 3.0L V6, 184–190 bhp). Check your VIN or registration year. The code is stamped on the engine block.

items.question: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain issue, or does it need replacement?
items.answer: Yes — if caught early. Timing chain and guide replacement costs £1,500–£3,500 and is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement (£3,000–£5,500). A 2014 recall addressed timing chain tensioner oil leaks in some OM651 engines. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately — ignored chain wear can lead to catastrophic engine damage. The timing chain should be inspected regularly after 200,000 kilometres for signs of wear or noise.
